title: What is an Outlier?
concept: An outlier is a data point that sits far outside the normal range of the rest of the data, potentially distorting machine learning models.

exercises.prompt: Warmup: How does Robust Scaling prevent extreme outliers from distorting feature scales?

options.text: It scales features using the median and Interquartile Range (IQR) instead of mean and standard deviation
It deletes all outlier rows from the dataset automatically
It rounds all values to the nearest integer

exercises.explain: Exactly! Robust scaling uses the median and IQR because they are not affected by extreme values. But how do we define what an outlier actually is?

cards.title: A giant walks in
cards.body: If nine people who are 5'10" stand in a room and a 9-foot giant walks in, the average height instantly jumps. One extreme individual has distorted the representation of the whole group.

cards.title: What is an Outlier?
cards.body: An outlier is an observation point that lies an abnormal distance from other values in a random sample from a population. It sits far outside the normal pattern.

cards.title: The megaphone effect
cards.body: Imagine a choir where everyone sings at a normal speaking volume, except for one person who screams through a megaphone. The megaphone user is the outlier that disrupts the balance.

cards.title: Mansion vs. Suburbia
cards.body: In a suburb where most homes cost between $200k and $500k, a single $15 million historic mansion is an outlier. It drags the average home price way up, misrepresenting the typical home.

cards.title: Outliers aren't always errors
cards.body: While typos (like typing 1000 instead of 10) cause outliers, they can also represent genuine, highly valuable extreme events—like a billionaire's wealth or credit card fraud.

cards.title: Mean vs. Median
cards.body: How outliers affect mean and median differently in a dataset of [1, 2, 2, 3, 100]:

left.text:
Value: 21.6
Heavily distorted
Does not represent the majority

exercises.prompt: Which statistical metric is most heavily distorted when a dataset contains extreme outliers?
exercises.explain: The mean is calculated by summing all values and dividing by the count, so a single massive outlier will pull the mean toward it.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An outlier in a dataset is always a mistake or corrupted data that must be deleted.
exercises.explain: False! Outliers can be errors, but they can also be real, important data points—like a billionaire's income or credit card fraud.

exercises.sentence: A data point that sits far outside the normal range of a dataset is called an ___.
exercises.explain: An outlier is a value that deviates significantly from the rest of the observations in the dataset.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following scenarios describes a real-world outlier?

options.text: A temperature reading of 120°F in Antarctica
A temperature reading of 72°F in California during spring
A temperature reading of 32°F in New York during winter

exercises.explain: A reading of 120°F in Antarctica is extremely far from normal expectations and is a clear outlier, likely caused by a sensor error.

exercises.prompt: You are training a model to predict delivery times. One package took 15 days because of a rare customs delay, while all others took 1-3 days. If you keep the 15-day package in your training data using standard mean-based loss, how will it affect the model?

options.text: The model's predictions will be pulled upward, overestimating delivery times for normal packages.
The model will ignore the 15-day package completely.
The model's predictions will be pulled downward to compensate.
The model will become 100% accurate for all packages.

exercises.explain: Correct! The extreme outlier drags the mean-based loss function, forcing the model to overestimate normal delivery times just to reduce the error on that single outlier. Magnificent job completing this lesson on what outliers are!
